This contains the `run-` prefix which I think is not desirable. Ideally you could do this in one step
```bash
...
--checkpoint_dir "$XDG_DATA_HOME/$(basename -s .sh ${0#run-})" \
...
```
But that doesn't seem to work due to some bash foo I don't have.

So one can do it in two steps.
```bash
XDG_DATA_HOME=...
CHECKPOINT_DIR=$(basename ${0%.*})
CHECKPOINT_DIR=${CHECKPOINT_DIR#run-}
...
   --validation_step 5 \
   --checkpoint_dir "$XDG_DATA_HOME/$CHECKPOINT_DIR" \
...
```

As written, these should be in a catch_warnings block too.  In python 3, the warnings get emitted every time, not just the first time, so these three makePSF calls emit warnings.  (In python 2, the first one is emitted, then gets ignored by the filter.  Then later passes over the warning don't get emitted.)

There are some in later tests too.  You can add some print statements in python 2.7 to see when it would happen -- "start ignore warnings", "end ignore warnings" here, and then "emitting warning" in the code where the warning is issued.  Or temporarily change the warning to a print statement.

The idea is interesting but the implementation is poor. See [this RedHat post](https://developers.redhat.com/blog/2017/04/04/openjdk-and-containers/):
> Specifying -Xmx=1g is telling the JVM to allocate a 1gb heap. It’s not telling the JVM to limit its entire memory usage to 1gb. There are card tables, code caches, and all sorts of other off heap data structures.

Even by specifying `-XX:MaxRAM` I don't think that it's wise to instruct the tool to use all RAM in `requirements.resources.memory` up to the very last byte. There is likely some overhead by the shell etc. as well. @tjanez may want to contribute here.

Yes. It needs two separate processes to lock reliably.

Long story short, locks in *nix suck a lot more than they could reliably suck. A common bad idea that locks are per process+file rather than fd. Which means that if you got two fds for the same file, lock may be shared by them and locking ops on one of them affect the other. So two `LOCK_EX` attempt may succeed (because the file is already locked exclusively), and — the real fun thing — closing one of the fds may actually release the lock on both.

I briefly discussed such an API with @hachikuji about six months ago, but we didnt really decide on anything.

There are two forms that UBCevents Calendar uses for their start and end times: if it is a same day event, it has the form of `Saturday, August 12, 2017 9:00 AM - 1:00 PM`, and if it is multi-day, `Saturday, August 12, 2017 9:00 AM - Sunday, August 13, 2017 1:00 PM`. I first check for the multi-day case, and if that fails (i.e. when you can't parse an entire datetime from just a time), it combines the date of the `start_time` and the time of the `end_time`.

Sadly (because it would have been very nice) this will not work to set Slice() in the cases where the `value` node is a `Subscript()` node. Because the ending locations of `Subscript()` nodes are set from the child nodes. This sequence should explain what I am trying to say:
-- Subscript() ending locations are set from child node,
-- Slice() is a child of Subscript(),
-- Slice() needs to be set before Subscript(),
-- therefore the ending position of the parent's `value` node (in the cases where the value node is a Subscript()) cannot be used to set the Slice() ending locations..

```
...
┃  ┃  ┃━ Subscript(), name: None,  value: Subscript()                 💥💥💥
┃  ┃  ┃  fromlineno:       15 ... end_lineno:       15
┃  ┃  ┃  col_offset:        0 ... end_col_offset:    7
┃  ┃  ┃  >>>n[:][:]    <============== cannot use end_col_offset here though :-(
┃  ┃  ┃  >>>n[:][:]
┃  ┃  ┃  ┃━ Subscript(), name: None,  value: Name(n)                 💥💥💥
┃  ┃  ┃  ┃  fromlineno:       15 ... end_lineno:       15
┃  ┃  ┃  ┃  col_offset:        0 ... end_col_offset:    4
┃  ┃  ┃  ┃  >>>n[:]
┃  ┃  ┃  ┃  >>>n[:]
┃  ┃  ┃  ┃  ┃━ Name(n), name: n,  value: None
┃  ┃  ┃  ┃  ┃  fromlineno:       15 ... end_lineno:       15
┃  ┃  ┃  ┃  ┃  col_offset:        0 ... end_col_offset:    1
┃  ┃  ┃  ┃  ┃  >>>n
┃  ┃  ┃  ┃  ┃  >>>n
┃  ┃  ┃  ┃  ┃━  Slice(), name: None,  value: None
┃  ┃  ┃  ┃  ┃  fromlineno:       15 ... end_lineno:       15
┃  ┃  ┃  ┃  ┃  col_offset:        2 ... end_col_offset:    3
┃  ┃  ┃  ┃  ┃  >>>:
┃  ┃  ┃  ┃  ┃  >>>:
┃  ┃  ┃  ┃━ Slice(), name: None,  value: None                 💥💥💥
┃  ┃  ┃  ┃  fromlineno:       15 ... end_lineno:       15
┃  ┃  ┃  ┃  col_offset:        5 ... end_col_offset:    6
┃  ┃  ┃  ┃  >>>:    <============== want to set the col_offset, end_col_offset.
┃  ┃  ┃  ┃  >>>:
...
```
